ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Mee Siam. FOODON_03311069 (rice noodle dish) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (rice noodles, cooked) is the closest parent. No specific Mee Siam composite dish code exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for Mee Siam or a sufficiently close Singaporean noodle dish composite.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of Mee Siam Special as served at Qi Ji, approximately 450 g, comprising brown rice vermicelli, tau pok, hard-boiled egg, bean sprouts, spicy-sour gravy, crispy chicken cutlet, and begedil.

nutrient_source.title: Mee Siam (HPB Singapore Foodo / Energy and Nutrient Composition of Food)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Foodo lists Mee Siam (standard, white rice vermicelli) at approximately 130 kcal per 100 g. This Qi Ji variant uses brown rice vermicelli and adds crispy chicken cutlet and begedil, increasing energy, protein, and fat. Values have been adapted upward from the HPB Mee Siam baseline to reflect the additional protein-rich and fried components. Per-100g values are estimated for the composite dish as served (approximately 450 g plate). USDA SR Legacy data for chicken breast (fried, coated) and potato patty were used to calibrate the protein and fat uplift. Confidence is medium due to the composite estimation.

gi_source.title: Rice vermicelli, boiled — GI value from International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Mee Siam Special as a composite dish. The GI of 53 is derived from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for boiled rice vermicelli (GI ~53, low category), which is the primary carbohydrate vehicle in this dish. Brown rice vermicelli has a similar or slightly lower GI than white rice vermicelli; GI 53 is therefore a conservative upper-bound estimate. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (53 × 80.1) / 100 ≈ 42.5, where carbohydrate per 450 g serving = 17.8 × 4.5 = 80.1 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from spicy-sour gravy and shrimp paste
hypertension.guidance: Each 450 g serving provides approximately 1395 mg sodium, representing over 60% of the recommended daily limit of 2000 mg for hypertensive individuals; patients should request reduced gravy or consume only half the gravy to lower sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from rice vermicelli carbohydrates
type2_diabetes.guidance: The estimated glycemic load of approximately 42.5 per serving is high for a single meal; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consider a half-portion of noodles, pair with the protein components (chicken cutlet, egg, tau pok) to blunt postprandial glucose rise, and monitor blood glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat and sodium from fried chicken cutlet and gravy
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The crispy chicken cutlet and begedil contribute saturated fat (~6.3 g per serving) and the gravy contributes significant sodium; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion frequency to once or twice per week and opt for reduced gravy to manage both saturated fat and sodium load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mee Siam Special is a Singaporean hawker-style noodle dish built on a foundation of thin rice vermicelli (bee hoon) served in or alongside a spicy, tangy, and slightly sweet gravy made with dried shrimp, shrimp paste (belacan), tamarind, and chilli. The Qi Ji restaurant version uses brown rice vermicelli, which provides a slightly nuttier flavour and marginally higher fibre content than conventional white rice vermicelli. The dish is garnished with tau pok (fried tofu puffs that absorb the gravy), a halved hard-boiled egg, fresh bean sprouts for crunch, and a wedge of calamansi lime for acidity. The 'Special' designation at Qi Ji adds a crispy breaded chicken cutlet and a begedil (a pan-fried spiced potato patty of Malay-Peranakan origin), making it a more substantial and protein-rich meal than standard Mee Siam. Nutritionally, the dish provides moderate energy (~666 kcal per 450 g plate), meaningful protein (~37 g) from the chicken, egg, and tofu, and a moderate fat content elevated by the fried components. Sodium is a primary clinical concern due to the shrimp-paste-based gravy. The glycemic index of the rice vermicelli base is low to medium (~53), but the glycemic load per full serving is high due to portion size. Mee Siam has Malay and Peranakan (Nyonya) culinary roots and is widely consumed across Singapore as a breakfast or lunch dish. It is distinct from Thai-style Pad Thai despite superficial similarities in the noodle format.

confidence_reason: HPB Foodo provides a Mee Siam baseline for standard white rice vermicelli version. This Qi Ji Special variant adds crispy chicken cutlet and begedil, requiring upward adaptation of protein, fat, and energy values using USDA SR Legacy component data. No exact composite dish match exists in any approved primary source for this specific restaurant variant. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source database for this dish category. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source database for this dish category. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and trans fat values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy component data for fried chicken and potato patty and are approximate. The serving size of 450 g is an estimate based on typical Qi Ji Mee Siam Special plate weight; no official declared weight was available from the restaurant. Glycemic load per serving is calculated using estimated carbohydrate content per 450 g serving. The halal status reflects Qi Ji's general halal certification; consumers should verify current certification status directly with the restaurant. The dish contains shrimp paste (belacan) which is a shellfish-derived allergen.
